subject: Physics
question_text: In Rutherford’s nuclear model of the atom, the nucleus (radius about 10 –15 m) is analogous to the sun about which the electron move in orbit (radius {tex}\approx {/tex} 10 –10 m) like the earth orbits around the sun. If the dimensions of the solar system had the same proportions as those of the atom, would the earth be closer to or farther away from the sun than actually, it is? The radius of the earth’s orbit is about 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 11 m. The radius of the sun is taken as 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m.
question_html: In Rutherford’s nuclear model of the atom, the nucleus (radius about 10–15 m) is analogous to the sun about which the electron move in orbit (radius {tex}\approx {/tex}10–10 m) like the earth orbits around the sun. If the dimensions of the solar system had the same proportions as those of the atom, would the earth be closer to or farther away from the sun than actually, it is? The radius of the earth’s orbit is about 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 m. The radius of the sun is taken as 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m.
solution_text: The ratio of the radius of electron’s orbit to the radius of a nucleus is (10 –10 m)/(10 –15 m) = 10 5 , that is, the radius of the electron’s orbit is 10 5 times larger than the radius of the nucleus. If the radius of the earth’s orbit around the sun were 10 5 times larger than the radius of the sun, the radius of the earth’s orbit would be 10 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 13 m. This is more than 100 times greater than the actual orbital radius of the earth. Thus, the earth would be much farther away from the sun. It implies that an atom contains a much greater fraction of empty space than our solar system does.
solution_html: The ratio of the radius of electron’s orbit to the radius of a nucleus is (10–10 m)/(10–15 m) = 105, that is, the radius of the electron’s orbit is 105 times larger than the radius of the nucleus. If the radius of the earth’s orbit around the sun were 105 times larger than the radius of the sun, the radius of the earth’s orbit would be 105 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1013 m. This is more than 100 times greater than the actual orbital radius of the earth. Thus, the earth would be much farther away from the sun. It implies that an atom contains a much greater fraction of empty space than our solar system does.

subject: Physics
question_text: In a Geiger-Marsden experiment, what is the distance of closest approach to the nucleus of a 7.7 MeV {tex}\alpha - {/tex} particle before it comes momentarily to rest and reverses its direction?
question_html: In a Geiger-Marsden experiment, what is the distance of closest approach to the nucleus of a 7.7 MeV {tex}\alpha - {/tex}particle before it comes momentarily to rest and reverses its direction?
solution_text: The key idea here is that throughout the scattering process, the total mechanical energy of the system consisting of an α-particle and a gold nucleus is conserved. The system’s initial mechanical energy is E i , before the particle and nucleus interact, and it is equal to its mechanical energy E f when the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle momentarily stops. The initial energy E i is just the kinetic energy K of the incoming {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. The final energy E f is just the electric potential energy U of the system. The potential energy U can be calculated from Eq. Let d be the centre-to-centre distance between the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle and the gold nucleus when the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle is at its stopping point. Then we can write the conservation of energy E i = E f as {tex}K=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(2 e)(Z e)}{d}=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}{/tex} Thus the distance of closest approach d is given by:- {tex}d=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} K}{/tex} The maximum kinetic energy found in α-particles of natural origin is 7.7 MeV or 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –12 J. Since {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 N m 2 /C 2 . Therefore with e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 C, Z = 79 we have, {tex}d=\frac{(2)\left(9.0 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)^{2} \mathrm{79}}{1.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{J}}{/tex} d (Au) = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –14 m = 30 fm. (1 fm (i.e. fermi) = 10 –15 m.) The radius of gold nucleus is, therefore, less than 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –14 m. This is not in very good agreement with the observed result as the actual radius of gold nucleus is 6 fm. The cause of discrepancy is that the distance of closest approach is considerably larger than the sum of the radii of the gold nucleus and the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. Thus, the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle reverses its motion without ever actually touching the gold nucleus.
solution_html:
The key idea here is that throughout the scattering process, the total mechanical energy of the system consisting of an α-particle and a gold nucleus is conserved. The system’s initial mechanical energy is Ei, before the particle and nucleus interact, and it is equal to its mechanical energy Ef when the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle momentarily stops. The initial energy Ei is just the kinetic energy K of the incoming {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle. The final energy Ef is just the electric potential energy U of the system. The potential energy U can be calculated from Eq. Let d be the centre-to-centre distance between the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle and the gold nucleus when the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle is at its stopping point. Then we can write the conservation of energy Ei = Ef as
{tex}K=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(2 e)(Z e)}{d}=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}{/tex}
Thus the distance of closest approach d is given by:-
{tex}d=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} K}{/tex}
The maximum kinetic energy found in α-particles of natural origin is 7.7 MeV or 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–12 J. Since {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 N m2/C2. Therefore with e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–19 C, Z = 79 we have,
{tex}d=\frac{(2)\left(9.0 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)^{2} \mathrm{79}}{1.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{J}}{/tex}
d (Au) = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–14 m = 30 fm. (1 fm (i.e. fermi) = 10–15 m.)
The radius of gold nucleus is, therefore, less than 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–14 m. This is not in very good agreement with the observed result as the actual radius of gold nucleus is 6 fm. The cause of discrepancy is that the
distance of closest approach is considerably larger than the sum of the radii of the gold nucleus and the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle. Thus, the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle reverses its motion without ever actually touching the gold nucleus.

subject: Physics
question_text: It is found experimentally that 13.6 eV energy is required to separate a hydrogen atom into a proton and an electron. Compute the orbital radius and the velocity of the electron in a hydrogen atom.
question_html: It is found experimentally that 13.6 eV energy is required to separate a hydrogen atom into a proton and an electron. Compute the orbital radius and the velocity of the electron in a hydrogen atom.
solution_text: Total energy of the electron in hydrogen atom is –13.6 eV = –13.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 J = –2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –18 J. Thus from Eq., we have {tex}-\frac{e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} = E This gives the orbital radius {tex}r=-\frac{e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} E}=-\frac{\left(9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{N} \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)^{2}}{(2)\left(-2.2 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{J}\right)}{/tex} = 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –11 m The velocity of the revolving electron can be computed from Eq. with m = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –31 kg, {tex}{1\over 2 }mv^2 ={e^2\over 4\pi\epsilon_0 r^2}{/tex} thus velocity of electron is given by :- {tex}v=\frac{e}{\sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} m r^2}}=2.2 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
solution_html:
Total energy of the electron in hydrogen atom is –13.6 eV = –13.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–19 J = –2.2 {tex}\times{/tex}10–18 J.
Thus from Eq., we have
{tex}-\frac{e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex}= E
This gives the orbital radius
{tex}r=-\frac{e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} E}=-\frac{\left(9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{N} \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)^{2}}{(2)\left(-2.2 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{J}\right)}{/tex}
= 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–11 m
The velocity of the revolving electron can be computed from Eq. with m = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–31 kg,

{tex}{1\over 2 }mv^2 ={e^2\over 4\pi\epsilon_0 r^2}{/tex} thus velocity of electron is given by :-
{tex}v=\frac{e}{\sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} m r^2}}=2.2 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: According to the classical electromagnetic theory, calculate the initial frequency of the light emitted by the electron revolving around a proton in hydrogen atom.
question_html: According to the classical electromagnetic theory, calculate the initial frequency of the light emitted by the electron revolving around a proton in hydrogen atom.
solution_text: We know that the velocity of an electron moving around a proton in a hydrogen atom in an orbit of radius is 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –11 m is 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m/s. Thus, the frequency of the electron moving around the proton is given by ; {tex}v=\frac{v}{2 \pi r}=\frac{2.2 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}}{2 \pi\left(5.3 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\approx {/tex} 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz According to the classical electromagnetic theory, we know that the frequency of the electromagnetic waves emitted by the revolving electrons is equal to the frequency of its revolution around the nucleus. Thus the initial frequency of the light emitted is given by 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz.
solution_html:
We know that the velocity of an electron moving around a proton in a hydrogen atom in an orbit of radius is 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–11 m is 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m/s. Thus, the frequency of the electron moving around the proton is given by ;
{tex}v=\frac{v}{2 \pi r}=\frac{2.2 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}}{2 \pi\left(5.3 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\approx {/tex} 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 Hz
According to the classical electromagnetic theory, we know that the frequency of the electromagnetic waves emitted by the revolving electrons is equal to the frequency of its revolution around the nucleus. Thus the initial frequency of the light emitted is given by 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 Hz.

subject: Physics
question_text: Suppose you are given a chance to repeat the alpha particle scattering experiment using a thin sheet of solid hydrogen in place of the gold foil. (Hydrogen is a solid at temperatures below 14 K). What results do you expect?
question_html: Suppose you are given a chance to repeat the alpha particle scattering experiment using a thin sheet of solid hydrogen in place of the gold foil. (Hydrogen is a solid at temperatures below 14 K). What results do you expect?
solution_text: The nucleus of a hydrogen atom is a proton (mass {tex}1.67 \times {10^{ - 27}}kg{/tex} ) which has only about one-fourth of the mass of an alpha particle {tex}\left( {6.64 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}kg} \right){/tex} . Because the alpha particle is more massive, it won't bounce back in even a head on collision with a proton. It is like a bowling ball colliding with a ping pong ball at rest. Thus, there would be no large angle scattering in this case. In Rutherford's experiment, by contrast, there was large angle scattering because a gold nucleus is more massive than an alpha particle. The analogy there is a ping pong ball hitting a bowling ball at rest.
solution_html: The nucleus of a hydrogen atom is a proton (mass {tex}1.67 \times {10^{ - 27}}kg{/tex}) which has only about one-fourth of the mass of an alpha particle {tex}\left( {6.64 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}kg} \right){/tex}. Because the alpha particle is more massive, it won't bounce back in even a head on collision with a proton. It is like a bowling ball colliding with a ping pong ball at rest. Thus, there would be no large angle scattering in this case. In Rutherford's experiment, by contrast, there was large angle scattering because a gold nucleus is more massive than an alpha particle. The analogy there is a ping pong ball hitting a bowling ball at rest.

subject: Physics
question_text: A difference of 2.3 eV separates two energy levels in an atom. What is the frequency of radiation emitted when the atom make a transition from the upper level to the lower level?
question_html: A difference of 2.3 eV separates two energy levels in an atom. What is the frequency of radiation emitted when the atom make a transition from the upper level to the lower level?
solution_html:
Here it is given that, E​​​​​​2​​​​​ - E​​​1 = 2.3 eV {tex} = 2.3 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex}
{tex}\nu = \frac{{{E_2} - {E_1}}}{h}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \nu = \frac{{2.3 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}{/tex}
or {tex}\nu = \frac{{3.68 \times {{10}^{ 15}}}}{{6.6 }}{/tex}
{tex} = 0.557 \times {10^{15}}Hz{/tex}
{tex} = 5.6 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. What are the kinetic and potential energies of the electron in this state?
question_html: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. What are the kinetic and potential energies of the electron in this state?
solution_text: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. Given, total ground state energy = -13.6 eV We know that, Kinetic energy = - Total Energy = - (-13.6 eV) = 13.6 eV and Potential energy = 2 (TE) = 2 {tex} \times {/tex} (-13.6 ) = - 27.2 eV
solution_html:
The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV.
Given, total ground state energy = -13.6 eV
We know that,
Kinetic energy = - Total Energy = - (-13.6 eV) = 13.6 eV
and
Potential energy = 2 (TE) = 2 {tex} \times {/tex} (-13.6 ) = - 27.2 eV

subject: Physics
question_text: A hydrogen atom initially in the ground level absorbs a photon, which excites it to the n = 4 level. Determine the wavelength and frequency of photon.
question_html: A hydrogen atom initially in the ground level absorbs a photon, which excites it to the n = 4 level. Determine the wavelength and frequency of photon.
solution_html:
Energy of an electron in nth orbit of H atom,
{tex}{E_n} = \frac{{ - 13.6}}{{{n^2}}}eV{/tex}
Thus, E1 = -13.6 eV
Energy in 4th (n = 4) level,
{tex}{E_4} = \frac{{ - 13.6}}{{{4^2}}} = - 0.85{/tex} eV
Now, {tex}\Delta E = {E_4} - {E_1}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta E = - 0.85 - ( - 13.6)eV{/tex}
{tex}\Delta E = 12.75eV{/tex}
Thus, h{tex}\nu{/tex} = 12.75 eV
{tex}h\nu = 12.75 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex}
{tex}\nu = \frac{{12.75 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\nu = 3.078 \times {10^{15}}Hz{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\lambda = \frac{c}{\nu } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{3.078 \times {{10}^{15}}}}{/tex}
So, {tex}\lambda = 974.4\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: Using the Bohr's model, calculate the speed of the electron in a hydrogen atom in the n = 1, 2 and 3 levels. Calculate the orbital period in each of these levels.
question_html:
1. Using the Bohr's model, calculate the speed of the electron in a hydrogen atom in the n = 1, 2 and 3 levels.
  2. Calculate the orbital period in each of these levels.
solution_text: Now, {tex}v = \frac{c}{n}\alpha {/tex} , where {tex}\alpha = \frac{{2\pi K{e^2}}}{{ch}}{/tex} = 0.0073 {tex}{v_1} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{1} \times 0.0073 = 2.19 \times {10^6}m/s{/tex} {tex}{v_2} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{3} \times 0.0073 = 1.095 \times {10^6}m/s{/tex} {tex}{v_3} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{3} \times 0.0073 = 7.3 \times {10^5}m/s{/tex} Orbital period, {tex}T = \frac{{2\pi r}}{\upsilon }{/tex} As {tex}{r_1} = 0.53 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex} {tex}{T_1} = \frac{{2\pi \times 0.53 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{2.19 \times {{10}^6}}} = 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s{/tex} As r 2 = 4 r 1 and {tex}{\upsilon _2} = \frac{1}{2}{\upsilon _1}{/tex} {tex}{T_2} = 8\;{T_1} = 8 \times 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s = 1.216 \times {10^{ - 15}}s{/tex} As r 3 = 9r 1 and {tex}{\upsilon _3} = \frac{1}{3}{\upsilon _1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {T_3} = 27{/tex} {tex}{T_1} = 27 \times 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s = 4.1 \times {10^{ - 15}}s{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Now, {tex}v = \frac{c}{n}\alpha {/tex},
    where {tex}\alpha = \frac{{2\pi K{e^2}}}{{ch}}{/tex} = 0.0073
    {tex}{v_1} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{1} \times 0.0073 = 2.19 \times {10^6}m/s{/tex}
    {tex}{v_2} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{3} \times 0.0073 = 1.095 \times {10^6}m/s{/tex}
    {tex}{v_3} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{3} \times 0.0073 = 7.3 \times {10^5}m/s{/tex}
  2. Orbital period, {tex}T = \frac{{2\pi r}}{\upsilon }{/tex}
    As {tex}{r_1} = 0.53 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex}
    {tex}{T_1} = \frac{{2\pi \times 0.53 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{2.19 \times {{10}^6}}} = 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s{/tex}
    As r2 = 4 r1 and {tex}{\upsilon _2} = \frac{1}{2}{\upsilon _1}{/tex}
    {tex}{T_2} = 8\;{T_1} = 8 \times 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s = 1.216 \times {10^{ - 15}}s{/tex}
    As r3 = 9r1 and {tex}{\upsilon _3} = \frac{1}{3}{\upsilon _1}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {T_3} = 27{/tex} {tex}{T_1} = 27 \times 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s = 4.1 \times {10^{ - 15}}s{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: The radius of the innermost electron orbit of a hydrogen atom is 5.3 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -11 m. What are the radii of the n = 2 and n = 3 orbits?
question_html: The radius of the innermost electron orbit of a hydrogen atom is 5.3 {tex} \times {/tex}10-11 m. What are the radii of the n = 2 and n = 3 orbits?
solution_text: Given, the radius of the innermost orbit, r 1 = 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –11 m Then, let r 2 be the radius of an orbit at n = 2. Then, r 2 = (n) 2 r 1 As shown in the diagram: {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r 2 = 2 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –11 m {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r 2 = 2.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –10 m For n = 3, we can write the corresponding electron radius as: {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r 3 = (n) 2 r 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r 3 = 3 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –11 m {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r 3 = 4.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –10 m Hence, the radii of an electron for n = 2 and n = 3 orbits are 2.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –10 m and 4.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –10 m
solution_html:
Given, the radius of the innermost orbit, r1 = 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–11 m
Then, let r2 be the radius of an orbit at n = 2.
Then, r2 = (n)2r1
As shown in the diagram:

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–11 m
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = 2.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–10 m
For n = 3, we can write the corresponding electron radius as:
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r3 = (n)2 r1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r3 = 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–11 m
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r3 = 4.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–10 m
Hence, the radii of an electron for n = 2 and n = 3 orbits are 2.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–10 m and 4.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–10 m

subject: Physics
question_text: A 12.5 eV electron beam is used to bombard gaseous hydrogen at room temperature. What series of wavelengths will be emitted?
question_html: A 12.5 eV electron beam is used to bombard gaseous hydrogen at room temperature. What series of wavelengths will be emitted?
solution_text: Given energy of electron beam E = {tex}12.5 \mathrm{eV}=12.5 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex} . Also, the energy of the gaseous hydrogen in its ground state at room temperature is −13.6 eV. When gaseous hydrogen is bombarded with an electron beam, the energy of the gaseous hydrogen becomes = −13.6 + 12.5 eV i.e., −1.1 eV. Thus, the electrons of hydrogen will be raised to the third orbit, because this energy is approximately equal to the energy of gaseous hydrogen(-1.5eV). Now, the following transitions are possible n = 3 to n = 2 then n = 2 to n = 1 or n = 3 to n = 1 Therefore, three spectral lines will be obtained. These are {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.097 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\lambda=6.56 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}{/tex} or 656 nm. This line lies in the Balmer series {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.097 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\lambda=1.22 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{m}{/tex} or 122 nm. This line lies in the Lyman series. {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.097 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\lambda=1.03 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{m}{/tex} or 103 nm. This line lies in the Lyman series.
solution_html:
Given energy of electron beam
E = {tex}12.5 \mathrm{eV}=12.5 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex}. Also, the energy of the gaseous hydrogen in its ground state at room temperature is −13.6 eV. When gaseous hydrogen is bombarded with an electron beam, the energy of the gaseous hydrogen becomes = −13.6 + 12.5 eV i.e., −1.1 eV.
Thus, the electrons of hydrogen will be raised to the third orbit, because this energy is approximately equal to the energy of gaseous hydrogen(-1.5eV). 
Now, the following transitions are possible
n = 3 to n = 2 then n = 2 to n = 1 or n = 3 to n = 1
Therefore, three spectral lines will be obtained. These are
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.097 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\lambda=6.56 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}{/tex} or 656 nm. This line lies in the Balmer series
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.097 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\lambda=1.22 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{m}{/tex} or 122 nm. This line lies in the Lyman series. 
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.097 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\lambda=1.03 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{m}{/tex} or 103 nm. This line lies in the Lyman series.

subject: Physics
question_text: In accordance with the Bohr’s model, find the quantum number that characterizes the earth’s revolution around the sun in an orbit of radius 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 11 m with orbital speed 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 m/s. (Mass of earth = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 24 kg.)
question_html: In accordance with the Bohr’s model, find the quantum number that characterizes the earth’s revolution around the sun in an orbit of radius 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 m with orbital speed 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 m/s. (Mass of earth = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 kg.)
solution_text: Angular momentum is given by the relation: {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{mvr}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex} Where M is the mass of Earth and is equal to 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 24 kg. v is the orbital speed of Earth = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 m/s r is the orbital radius of Earth = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 11 m n is the quantum number {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{mvr} 2 \pi}{\mathrm{h}}{/tex} substituting the values, we get {tex}\mathrm{n}=\frac{\left[6.0 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{Kg} \times 3 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{ms}^{-1} \times 1.5 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{m} \times 2 \times 3.14\right]}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J}-\mathrm{sec}}{/tex} on calculating, we get Thus, quanta number that characterizes the Earth’s revolution is n = 2.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 74
solution_html:
Angular momentum is given by the relation:
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{mvr}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex}
Where M is the mass of Earth and is equal to 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 kg.
v is the orbital speed of Earth = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 m/s
r is the orbital radius of Earth = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 m
n is the quantum number
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{mvr} 2 \pi}{\mathrm{h}}{/tex}
substituting the values, we get
{tex}\mathrm{n}=\frac{\left[6.0 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{Kg} \times 3 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{ms}^{-1} \times 1.5 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{m} \times 2 \times 3.14\right]}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J}-\mathrm{sec}}{/tex}
on calculating, we get
Thus, quanta number that characterizes the Earth’s revolution is n = 2.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1074
